fact: September 21 is almost always the exact moment when day and night become perfectly equal all around the world - it's called the autumn equinox in the north and spring equinox in the south!
On this date, the sun rises due east and sets due west no matter where you live on Earth - your compass and the sun are best friends today!
Monarch butterflies are typically migrating around this time in North America, flying up to 3,000 miles to Mexico in one of nature's most amazing journeys!
Tree leaves in the northern hemisphere are breaking down their green chlorophyll around this time, revealing hidden yellow and orange colors that were there all along - like nature's magic trick!
September 21 marks exactly 264 days since New Year's Day, which means there are exactly 101 days left in the year - pretty neat math!
Squirrels often enter busy gathering mode around this time in northern climates, burying thousands of nuts and seeds to prepare for winter - and they forget where they put many of them!
The Northern Lights often become more active near the equinox because Earth's magnetic field gets tilted just right to catch extra solar wind particles!
In Australia and other southern places, this is typically the first official day of spring - flowers are blooming while northern kids are seeing leaves change!
September 21 usually falls in the middle of harvest season when pumpkins, apples, and corn are at their peak ripeness on northern farms!
Bears in northern regions are often in hyperphagia around this time - they're eating up to 20,000 calories per day to get ready for hibernation, which is like eating 40 hamburgers daily!
Spiders often seem to appear everywhere around this date because they've been growing all summer and are now full-sized - it's spider season!
